architecture.summary: Prompt tuning learns soft prompt embeddings prepended to frozen T5 encoder inputs. A fixed sequence of special tokens has learnable embedding parameters (soft prompts) that are updated via backpropagation during task training, while all model weights remain frozen. The soft prompt is concatenated with embedded task input and flows through the standard T5 encoder-decoder as normal text-to-text generation.
trained_components: soft prompt embeddings (prepended input layer only)

biblio.title: The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning

claims.statement: Prompt tuning matches the strong performance of model tuning (where all model weights are tuned) as model size exceeds billions of parameters, closing the gap on SuperGLUE.
At XXL size (11 billion parameters), prompt tuning matches multi-task model tuning baseline despite having over 20,000× fewer task-specific parameters.
Prompt-tuned T5-Small matches GPT-3 XL (over 400× larger), and prompt-tuned T5-Large beats GPT-3 175B (over 100× larger) on SuperGLUE few-shot.

claims.dataset: TextbookQA (MRQA 2019 shared task, trained on SQuAD)
claims.statement: Prompt tuning outperforms model tuning on zero-shot domain transfer to TextbookQA with a +12.5 F1 gap, and shows larger gains in cases of larger domain shifts (e.g., to Biomedical and Textbooks).
Five-prompt ensemble trained on single frozen T5-XXL achieves 91.3 SuperGLUE score, exceeding both the single-prompt average (90.5) and the best individual prompt (91.0).

claims.dataset: SuperGLUE ablations across all model sizes
claims.statement: XXL model is robust to hyperparameter choices across prompt length, initialization strategy, and pre-training objective; largest model is most forgiving of non-ideal configurations.

T5 models pre-trained on span corruption objective show instability across model sizes (only 1 out of 5 models work well), with mid-sized models never learning to output legal class labels, copying sub-spans from input, or predicting empty strings; LM adaptation resolves this unreliability across all sizes.

Learned soft prompts show little interpretability as sequences, but nearest-neighbor analysis reveals tight semantic clustering (e.g., 'Technology' cluster, 'entirely/completely' cluster), suggesting prompts learn word-like representations; class-label initialization often persists after training.
